content.text: What it is

Screen reader testing for agentic AI output formats means systematically verifying that the structured content produced by AI models — tables, code blocks, lists, mathematical notation, and inline citations — is correctly rendered into accessible HTML and announced comprehensibly by assistive technologies. Agentic AI systems produce a much wider variety of output formats than traditional web applications, and the markdown-to-HTML rendering layer that bridges model output to the DOM is the critical accessibility control point.
Why it matters

Screen reader users interact with AI output differently from sighted users. A sighted user reading a six-row data table can scan it in two seconds; a screen reader user must navigate cell by cell using table-navigation keyboard shortcuts, relying on column headers to understand the meaning of each cell. If the AI's rendered table lacks thead and th elements, the screen reader cannot announce column headers and the table becomes incomprehensible. The same problem applies to code blocks (which must be in a pre > code element to be navigable as a region), numbered lists (which must use ol, not dash-prefixed plain text), and citations (which require aria-label to be distinguishable from body text).

As agentic AI expands from simple chat into complex multi-step workflows with rich output (reports, dashboards, structured data exports), the surface area for screen reader accessibility failures grows. Testing only the basic text response and ignoring structured output formats creates a two-tier experience where users with visual impairments cannot fully use the AI's most powerful capabilities.
Benefits
Ensures users dependent on screen readers can navigate AI tables, code blocks, and lists with the same efficiency as sighted users
Catches rendering failures where the markdown processor drops semantic structure (e.g., rendering header rows as plain text)
Validates ARIA region labelling for AI content types that do not have native HTML semantic equivalents
Prevents regression when the AI model updates to produce new output patterns or the markdown renderer is upgraded
Provides evidence for WCAG 2.1 Success Criteria 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) and 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) compliance
Trade-offs and limitations
AI output is non-deterministic: the model may produce a table in one response and a bullet list in the next for the same prompt
Full screen reader testing requires human testers with actual AT tools — automated DOM inspection cannot verify the auditory experience
Code block accessibility depends on the programming language syntax highlighter, which may strip semantic markup
Mathematical notation in AI output (LaTeX, MathML) requires specialised rendering that most markdown processors do not handle accessibly by default
Testing coverage is inherently incomplete because the full range of AI output formats cannot be enumerated in advance
How it works

Screen reader testing for AI output uses a two-layer approach. The first layer is automated DOM inspection: after a known prompt produces a known output type (table, code block, ordered list), assert that the DOM contains the correct semantic HTML. A test that sends 'Show me a comparison table of programming languages' and asserts the response HTML contains table > thead > tr > th elements catches the class of rendering failure where the AI produces markdown table syntax but the renderer drops the header row. This runs in CI on every PR.

The second layer is human AT testing: a tester using NVDA or VoiceOver navigates through a scripted set of AI responses covering all major output types. The tester follows a structured test script that records whether each output type was announced correctly: did the screen reader announce 'table with 4 columns and 6 rows' at the table entry point? Did it announce 'code block' when entering the pre element? Did it read the ordered list items in numbered sequence? This runs quarterly and before major releases.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Screen Reader Interaction with Agentic AI Output: from model through renderer to accessible DOM to screen reader

content.text: The renderer is the accessibility control point. The model produces markdown or plain text; the renderer must convert this to semantic HTML with appropriate ARIA attributes before it reaches the DOM. A renderer that passes markdown through to a raw string insertion bypasses all semantic structure and produces an inaccessible result regardless of model output quality.
When to use it

Run automated DOM structure tests on every PR that touches the markdown renderer, the AI response component, or the output format handling code. Run manual AT testing when a new output format is introduced (e.g., the model begins producing LaTeX for math, or a new citation format), before major releases, and quarterly as a regression check. Prioritise testing with NVDA/Firefox (Windows) and VoiceOver/Safari (macOS) as these represent the most common AT combinations among users with visual impairments.
How to implement
Identify all output types your AI system currently produces: text paragraphs, tables, ordered and unordered lists, code blocks, inline code, blockquotes, and images
For each output type, write an integration test that sends a prompt guaranteed to produce that type and asserts the correct DOM structure using JSDOM or Playwright
Run these tests in CI against the production renderer configuration, not a test stub
For the table test: assert thead, th, and tbody elements exist and that th count matches column count in each data row
For the code block test: assert the code element is inside a pre element with a correct lang attribute
For the list test: assert ol for numbered items and ul for unordered items, not div/p with dash or number prefixes
Write a human AT test script covering all output types and schedule quarterly AT testing sessions
Add aria-label attributes to code blocks identifying the programming language for screen reader announcement
Examples

# AT Test Script: AI Output Format Accessibility
## Version: 2025-Q3

### Setup
- Screen reader: NVDA 2024.x + Firefox (Windows) or VoiceOver + Safari (macOS)
- AI system: [product name] staging environment
- Tester: [name]

### Test 1: Table Navigation
1. Send prompt: "Show me a table comparing Python, Go, and Rust"
2. Wait for AI response
3. Navigate to the table with screen reader table navigation (NVDA: T key)
Expected: SR announces "table with 3 columns and 3 rows" or similar
4. Navigate to first cell (NVDA: Tab)
Expected: SR announces column header, then cell value
Result: [ ] Pass  [ ] Fail  Notes: ___

### Test 2: Code Block Navigation
1. Send prompt: "Write a Python function that returns the sum of a list"
2. Navigate to the code block (NVDA: navigate by landmark)
Expected: SR announces "code block" or similar region label
3. Read through code with arrow keys
Expected: Code is read character by character with correct pronunciation
Result: [ ] Pass  [ ] Fail  Notes: ___

### Test 3: Ordered List Navigation
1. Send prompt: "List the five steps to deploy a web application"
2. Navigate to the list
Expected: SR announces "list of 5 items" and numbers each item
Result: [ ] Pass  [ ] Fail  Notes: ___
Real-world example

An enterprise AI writing assistant was tested with NVDA before a major release. The testing revealed that the AI's most-used feature — inline suggestions shown as grey overlay text — was entirely invisible to screen readers because the suggestion was implemented as a CSS ::after pseudo-element with no ARIA equivalent. Screen reader users could not hear the suggestion, could not accept it, and did not know it existed. The fix was to add a visually hidden span with the suggestion text and aria-label 'suggestion: [text]' that appeared alongside the visual overlay, allowing screen reader users to Tab to it and hear the suggestion content.
Common mistakes
Testing only with automated tools and not conducting human AT testing: automated tools cannot hear what the screen reader actually announces
Using role='region' without an aria-label on AI output containers: unnamed regions are announced as 'region' with no context
Rendering code as a plain monospace-font span instead of a pre > code element: screen readers do not announce it as code
Using CSS to style ordered lists and removing the list semantics: list-style-type: none can cause some screen readers to stop announcing list context
Forgetting to test AT interaction with the streaming state: the partially-loaded response must be accessible, not just the final state
Not testing with mobile AT: VoiceOver/iOS and TalkBack/Android interact with the DOM differently from their desktop equivalents
Best practices
Add a role='article' and aria-label='AI response' to each AI message container so screen readers can navigate between turns using landmark navigation
Implement code block aria-label that includes the programming language: 'Python code block' rather than unnamed
Use an accessible markdown renderer (marked with ARIA hooks, or a custom renderer) rather than raw innerHTML insertion
Include streaming-state accessibility: as tokens arrive, ARIA live region polite announcements allow AT to track response progress without interruption
Write AT test scripts that any engineer can execute with a 30-minute training, reducing dependency on specialist accessibility testers
Treat every new AI output format (LaTeX math, Mermaid diagrams, CSV downloads) as an accessibility story requiring explicit acceptance criteria
Performance and scalability

DOM structure assertions in automated tests are fast (under 100 ms per test) and should not be batched or deferred. The main performance concern is the prompt generation step — each test sends a live prompt to the AI model to produce the target output type. Use VCR cassettes or model stubs for the output format tests so CI runs deterministically without LLM API calls. Store known-good AI responses for each output type in a fixture file and feed them directly to the renderer for DOM structure assertion, isolating the renderer test from model non-determinism.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
NVDA
Primary Windows screen reader
Free; most common AT for blind users on Windows
VoiceOver
Primary macOS/iOS screen reader
Built-in; critical for Apple device coverage
axe-playwright
Automated axe integration with Playwright
Checks rendered HTML for WCAG violations
JSDOM
Headless DOM for structure tests
Used in Jest-based DOM structure assertions
a11y-checker
CI-friendly axe wrapper
Returns structured JSON for programmatic thresholds
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Automated DOM structure tests (CI)
Fast; catches renderer regression
Cannot verify what AT actually announces
Human AT testing sessions
Validates real user experience
Slow; requires AT expertise; quarterly cadence
Third-party accessibility audit
Independent verification
Point-in-time; expensive; misses regression over time
User testing with AT users
Uncovers barriers beyond WCAG
Hardest to recruit; highest signal
Security and reliability

Screen reader test prompts that trigger specific AI output types (tables with real-seeming data, code with real APIs) must be reviewed to ensure they do not reveal internal API endpoints or internal data structures in test fixtures stored in the repository. Use generic placeholder data in AT test fixtures. Store human AT test results (pass/fail logs) for 12 months as accessibility compliance evidence; these logs may be requested in EU EAA compliance audits.
Handling mathematical and diagram output

Agentic AI systems increasingly produce LaTeX mathematical notation, Mermaid diagram syntax, and other specialised formats that require additional accessibility treatment beyond standard markdown rendering. LaTeX rendered as raw TeX syntax is unreadable to screen readers; it must be converted to MathML (for AT support) or to a plain English description. A formula like \(E = mc^2\) should produce MathML output that screen readers with MathPlayer support can interpret, and should fall back to an aria-label 'E equals m c squared' for screen readers without MathML support.

Mermaid diagram output — flowcharts, sequence diagrams, entity-relationship diagrams — presents a similar challenge. The SVG rendered by Mermaid does not include descriptive text by default. Add a visually hidden description element inside the SVG that describes the diagram's content and structure. For complex diagrams, this description should be co-generated by the AI: when producing a Mermaid diagram, also produce a one-paragraph plain-text description and inject it as the SVG title and desc elements.
Convert LaTeX to MathML using KaTeX or MathJax with accessibility extensions enabled
Add aria-label fallback for MathML in screen readers without MathPlayer: generate plain English descriptions of formulas
Inject AI-generated diagram descriptions as SVG title and desc elements for Mermaid and other diagram outputs
For CSV and structured data outputs, render an accessible summary table rather than a download link only
Test math and diagram output specifically with NVDA + MathPlayer and VoiceOver (which has built-in MathML support)
Prioritising AT testing coverage

A comprehensive AT test suite for all AI output types and all AT tool combinations is impractical for most teams. Prioritise coverage using a risk matrix: test the most frequently used output types first (text paragraphs and tables likely cover 80% of real-world usage), then the output types with the highest accessibility impact if broken (code blocks for technical users, tables for data-heavy features). Deprioritise rarely-produced output types and those with low impact if temporarily inaccessible.

Prioritise AT combinations by user base composition. If your analytics show 70% of AT-using users are on Windows, prioritise NVDA/Firefox over VoiceOver/Safari. If you serve a government sector with mandatory WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, prioritise the AT tools listed in the relevant government accessibility standard (e.g., JAWS on Windows is the standard in many US federal agency contexts). Document the tested AT combinations in the VPAT or accessibility conformance report.
Tier 1 (test every release): plain text paragraphs, ordered lists, unordered lists — highest frequency, lowest implementation complexity
Tier 2 (test quarterly): tables, code blocks, images with AI-generated alt text — high impact when broken
Tier 3 (test semi-annually): math notation, diagrams, citations, footnotes — lower frequency, requires specialist AT
Document the tested AT combinations (tool, version, OS) in the accessibility conformance report
Review tier assignments annually as AI output patterns evolve with model capability improvements
Key takeaways
The markdown-to-HTML renderer is the accessibility control point for AI output; every output type requires explicit accessible HTML mapping
Automated DOM structure tests catch renderer regressions; human AT testing validates what screen readers actually announce
Test every structured output type: tables need thead/th, code blocks need pre > code, lists need ol/ul, images need descriptive alt text
Use VCR cassettes for renderer tests to avoid LLM API calls in CI while still testing against realistic model output
Add language-annotated aria-label to code blocks, role and aria-label to AI response containers, and skip links in conversation history
Include AT testing coverage for streaming state: partial responses must be accessible, not just the final completed response
